title: index
content: MCP MCP in ConvEngine is the runtime control layer that lets the engine decide when to call tools, how to sequence them, how to record each observation, and how to turn those observations back into one final assistant response. The important part is not just "tool calling"; it is deterministic orchestration with audit visibility, rule hooks, and data driven control from ce_ tables. Use this section when you need the internal execution model: planner loops, tool execution, Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) investigation flow, audit stages, and real end to end examples. Quick navigation Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) at a glance Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) is the DB driven investigation layer inside MCP. It is designed for production support and debugging use cases where the user asks in business language and the engine has to translate that into safe, explainable database investigation steps. At a high level, Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) works like this: 1. The user asks a business question such as "show requests assigned then rejected in last 24 hours" or "why is there no disconnect id after submit?" 2. IntentResolutionStep and RulesStep route the conversation into the MCP diagnostic path. 3. McpToolStep starts the planner loop. 4. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) resolves the business case from ce_mcp_case_type and ce_mcp_case_signal . 5. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) loads ranked knowledge from the semantic graph, API flow catalog, live schema introspection, and overlay hints. 6. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) chooses a playbook from ce_mcp_playbook , loads its steps and transitions, and validates the graph before execution. 7. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) executes only approved query templates and derived steps, follows graph transitions, and resolves the final outcome from ce_mcp_outcome_rule . 8. The planner returns ANSWER , POST_AGENT_MCP rules can still react, and ResponseResolutionStep produces one final user facing response. Why Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) matters: The user does not need to know physical table names. The engine can use live Postgres schema discovery plus optional semantic hints. The investigation path is configurable in DB tables, not hardcoded in Java. Every step is auditable through MCP observations and normal ConvEngine audit stages. Execution is guarded: playbook validation must succeed before the Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) execute step is allowed. In this MCP section AST deterministic SQL runtime path.", "Use this for stage by stage semantic query architecture, AST model, and compiler/handler flow."], ", "DB driven knowledge graph and investigation runtime.", "Use this for the full Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) model and metadata tables."], ", "HTTP tool execution path.", "Use this when your tool talks to REST/SOAP endpoints."], ", "Planner loop execution graph.", "Use this when tracing the confirmation first MCP lifecycle."], ", "Mockey + demo integration example.", "Use this for the simplest end to end example."], ", "Loan application flow.", "Use this for a practical business flow with tools."], ", "Extended loan application flow.", "Use this for correction + confirmation behavior."], ", "Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) based production investigation example.", "Use this for the full Zapper diagnostic story."], ", "Semantic query runtime example over zapper transactional schema.", "Use this for retrieval join path AST SQL compile/execute summary tracing."], ]} / Two tool paths 1. ToolOrchestrationStep (direct tool mode) request driven via tool_request executes one tool runs POST_TOOL_EXECUTION rules writes context.mcp.toolExecution. 2. McpToolStep (planner loop mode) planner loop ( CALL_TOOL / ANSWER ) writes context.mcp.observations[] and context.mcp.finalAnswer runs POST_AGENT_MCP rules writes context.mcp.lifecycle. Guardrail blocked branch If the planner proposes a blocked next tool: mcp_status=GUARDRAIL_BLOCKED_NEXT_TOOL fallback final text is written to context.mcp.finalAnswer POST_AGENT_MCP rules still run response is still finalized by ce_response resolution Rule ready MCP metadata context.mcp.lifecycle includes: phase , status , outcome , finished blocked , error , errorMessage lastAction , lastToolCode , lastToolGroup , lastToolArgs toolExecuted finalAnswerDetermined (true when MCP final answer is set) toolExecutionAbrupted (true when loop stops due to max loop guard without final answer) toolExecutionAbruptionLimit (configured loop guard limit) context.mcp.toolExecution includes: phase , status , outcome , finished error , scopeMismatch , toolExecuted toolCode , toolGroup , meta , result , errorMessage JSON_PATH examples for ce_rule.match_pattern text $[?(@.context.mcp.lifecycle.finished == true && @.context.mcp.lifecycle.outcome == 'BLOCKED')] $[?(@.context.mcp.lifecycle.error == true)] $[?(@.context.mcp.toolExecution.phase == 'POST_TOOL_EXECUTION' && @.context.mcp.toolExecution.status == 'SUCCESS')] $[?(@.context.mcp.toolExecution.scopeMismatch == true)] Audit stages to watch Tool orchestration path: TOOL_ORCHESTRATION_REQUEST TOOL_ORCHESTRATION_RESULT TOOL_ORCHESTRATION_ERROR Planner MCP path: MCP_PLAN_LLM_INPUT MCP_PLAN_LLM_OUTPUT MCP_TOOL_CALL MCP_TOOL_RESULT MCP_TOOL_ERROR MCP_FINAL_ANSWER Rule phases around these paths: RULE_MATCH (McpToolStep) / RULE_NO_MATCH (McpToolStep) RULE_MATCH (ToolOrchestrationStep PostTool) / RULE_NO_MATCH (ToolOrchestrationStep PostTool) RULE_MATCH (AgentIntentResolver) / RULE_NO_MATCH (AgentIntentResolver) RULE_MATCH (RulesStep) / RULE_NO_MATCH (RulesStep) Scope enforcement ce_mcp_tool and ce_mcp_planner are scope validated at startup: null/blank scope rows are rejected intent_code must be ANY , UNKNOWN , or defined in ce_intent state_code must be ANY , UNKNOWN , or present in ce_rule.state_code

The important part is not just "tool calling"; it is deterministic orchestration with audit visibility, rule hooks, and data-driven control from ce_* tables. <Highlight type="info" title="What this section covers"> Use this section when you need the internal execution model: planner loops, tool execution, Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) investigation flow, audit stages, and real end-to-end examples. </Highlight> ## Quick navigation - [Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) at a glance](#database-knowledge-graph-dbkg-at-a-glance) - [In this MCP section](#in-this-mcp-section) - [Two tool paths](#two-tool-paths) - [Guardrail blocked branch](#guardrail-blocked-branch) - [Rule-ready MCP metadata](#rule-ready-mcp-metadata) - [JSON_PATH examples for ce_rule.match_pattern ](#json_path-examples-for-ce_rulematch_pattern) - [Audit stages to watch](#audit-stages-to-watch) - [Scope enforcement](#scope-enforcement) - [Basics](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/basics) - [Advanced Guide](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/advanced) - [Semantic Catalog](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/semantic-catalog) - [Semantic Query](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/semantic-query) - [DB Knowledge Graph](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/knowledge) - [HTTP Tool](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/http-tool) - [Deep Dive](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/deep-dive) - [Example 1](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example1) - [Example 2](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example2) - [Example 3](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example3) - [Example 4 - Zapper Disconnect](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example4) - [Example 5 - Semantic Query (Zapper)](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example5) ## Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) at a glance Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) is the DB-driven investigation layer inside MCP. It is designed for production support and debugging use cases where the user asks in business language and the engine has to translate that into safe, explainable database investigation steps. At a high level, Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) works like this: 1. The user asks a business question such as "show requests assigned then rejected in last 24 hours" or "why is there no disconnect id after submit?" 2. IntentResolutionStep and RulesStep route the conversation into the MCP diagnostic path. 3. McpToolStep starts the planner loop. 4. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) resolves the business case from ce_mcp_case_type and ce_mcp_case_signal . 5. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) loads ranked knowledge from the semantic graph, API flow catalog, live schema introspection, and overlay hints. 6. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) chooses a playbook from ce_mcp_playbook , loads its steps and transitions, and validates the graph before execution. 7. Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) executes only approved query templates and derived steps, follows graph transitions, and resolves the final outcome from ce_mcp_outcome_rule . 8. The planner returns ANSWER , POST_AGENT_MCP rules can still react, and ResponseResolutionStep produces one final user-facing response. Why Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) matters: - The user does not need to know physical table names. - The engine can use live Postgres schema discovery plus optional semantic hints. - The investigation path is configurable in DB tables, not hardcoded in Java. - Every step is auditable through MCP observations and normal ConvEngine audit stages. - Execution is guarded: playbook validation must succeed before the Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) execute step is allowed. <DbTable title="Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) high-level model" columns={["Layer", "What it stores", "Why it matters"]} rows={[ ["Case resolution", "case types + weighted signals", "Turns business wording into a structured investigation family."], ["Knowledge graph", "domain entities, systems, api flows, schema overlays, status dictionary, ID lineage", "Gives the planner business meaning on top of physical schema."], ["Playbook graph", "playbooks, steps, transitions, executor templates", "Defines the ["[DB Knowledge Graph](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/knowledge)", "DB-driven knowledge graph and investigation runtime.", "Use this for the full Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG) model and metadata tables."], ["[HTTP Tool](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/http-tool)", "HTTP tool execution path.", "Use this when your tool talks to REST/SOAP endpoints."], ["[Deep Dive](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/deep-dive)", "Planner-loop execution graph.", "Use this when tracing the confirmation-first MCP lifecycle."], ["[Example 1](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example1)", "Mockey + demo integration example.", "Use this for the simplest end-to-end example."], ["[Example 2](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example2)", "Loan application flow.", "Use this for a practical business flow with tools."], ["[Example 3](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example3)", "Extended loan application flow.", "Use this for correction + confirmation behavior."], ["[Example 4 - Zapper Disconnect](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example4)", "Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG)-based production investigation example.", "Use this for the full Zapper diagnostic story."], ["[Example 5 - Semantic Query (Zapper)](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example5)", "Semantic-query runtime example over zapper transactional schema.", "Use this for retrieval -> join path -> AST -> SQL compile/execute -> summary tracing."], ]} /> ## Two tool paths 1. ToolOrchestrationStep (direct tool mode) - request-driven via tool_request - executes one tool - runs POST_TOOL_EXECUTION rules - writes context.mcp.toolExecution.* 2. McpToolStep (planner loop mode) - planner loop ( CALL_TOOL / ANSWER ) - writes context.mcp.observations[] and context.mcp.finalAnswer - runs POST_AGENT_MCP rules - writes context.mcp.lifecycle.* ## Guardrail blocked branch If the planner proposes a blocked next tool: - mcp_status=GUARDRAIL_BLOCKED_NEXT_TOOL - fallback final text is written to context.mcp.finalAnswer - POST_AGENT_MCP rules still run - response is still finalized by ce_response resolution ## Rule-ready MCP metadata context.mcp.lifecycle includes: - phase , status , outcome , finished - blocked , error , errorMessage - lastAction , lastToolCode , lastToolGroup , lastToolArgs - toolExecuted - finalAnswerDetermined (true when MCP final answer is set) - toolExecutionAbrupted (true when loop stops due to max-loop guard without final answer) - toolExecutionAbruptionLimit (configured loop guard limit) context.mcp.toolExecution includes: - phase , status , outcome , finished - error , scopeMismatch , toolExecuted - toolCode , toolGroup , meta , result , errorMessage ## JSON_PATH examples for ce_rule.match_pattern text $[?(@.context.mcp.lifecycle.finished == true && @.context.mcp.lifecycle.outcome == 'BLOCKED')] $[?(@.context.mcp.lifecycle.error == true)] $[?(@.context.mcp.toolExecution.phase == 'POST_TOOL_EXECUTION' && @.context.mcp.toolExecution.status == 'SUCCESS')] $[?(@.context.mcp.toolExecution.scopeMismatch == true)] ## Audit stages to watch Tool orchestration path: - TOOL_ORCHESTRATION_REQUEST - TOOL_ORCHESTRATION_RESULT - TOOL_ORCHESTRATION_ERROR Planner MCP path: - MCP_PLAN_LLM_INPUT - MCP_PLAN_LLM_OUTPUT - MCP_TOOL_CALL - MCP_TOOL_RESULT - MCP_TOOL_ERROR - MCP_FINAL_ANSWER Rule phases around these paths: - RULE_MATCH (McpToolStep) / RULE_NO_MATCH (McpToolStep) - RULE_MATCH (ToolOrchestrationStep PostTool) / RULE_NO_MATCH (ToolOrchestrationStep PostTool) - RULE_MATCH (AgentIntentResolver) / RULE_NO_MATCH (AgentIntentResolver) - RULE_MATCH (RulesStep) / RULE_NO_MATCH (RulesStep) ## Scope enforcement ce_mcp_tool and ce_mcp_planner are scope-validated at startup: - null/blank scope rows are rejected - intent_code must be ANY , UNKNOWN , or defined in ce_intent - state_code must be ANY , UNKNOWN , or present in ce_rule.state_code

title: knowledge
content: MCP Knowledge (Database Knowledge Graph) This page is the database backed MCP knowledge model for ConvEngine. It documents the new dbkg. tool chain, the ce_ metadata package behind it, and the runtime safety model that makes the DB investigation flow configurable instead of hardcoded in Java. dbkg. is not a raw SQL generator. It is a DB driven investigation pipeline: resolve the case, load graph knowledge, choose a playbook, validate the playbook graph, then execute approved query steps. Story first: how Database Knowledge Graph works from a real user question Start from the operator, not the table. A real user does not know zp_ui_data_history , ce_mcp_query_template , or ce_mcp_playbook_transition . They speak in support language, and the engine has to do the translation. The story below is intentionally broken into real turn by turn cards so you can see exactly what happens before, during, and after the Database Knowledge Graph tool chain. Turn 0 , "assistantMessage": "Hi, how can I help you today?" } } Turn 1 } Turn 2 , "execution": }, "assistantMessage": "I found the request. The account location mapping exists, but there is no matching inventory row ..." } } Turn 2.1 , "assistantMessage": "It is tied to disconnect request ZPR1002 for UPS at the Irving account location link." } } Turn 2.2 , "assistantMessage": "The next place to check is the original provisioning or inventory sync path ..." } } Turn 3 , "execution": , "finalAnswer": "Found 1 request that moved from ASSIGNED to REJECTED in the last 24 hours: ZPR1001." } } } Turn 4 , "outcome": "INVENTORY_MISSING", "finalAnswer": "The request exists, but there is no matching inventory row ..." } } } Turn 5 , "outcome": "DOWNSTREAM_FAILURE_FOUND", "finalAnswer": "Found a stuck disconnect: ZPR1003 / DON9001 ..." } } } Turn 6 , "outcome": "BILLBANK_GAPS_FOUND", "finalAnswer": "Found 1 propagation gap: DON9002 / ZPDISC7002 exists in the order domain but is missing in BillBank." } } } What this story proves rejected diagnosis.", "The full Database Knowledge Graph tool chain executes and returns a real result."], ["3", "A concrete inventory gap diagnosis.", "Database Knowledge Graph chains multiple query steps using runtime bound params."], ["4", "A concrete downstream failure diagnosis.", "Database Knowledge Graph uses a time derived step plus an approved trace query."], ["5", "A concrete BillBank propagation gap diagnosis.", "Database Knowledge Graph reconciles across systems without the user naming the tables."] ]} / Database Knowledge Graph runtime path This is the complete end to end Database Knowledge Graph path for one investigative turn. Each step below uses the same lifecycle style chip presentation as the deep dive pipeline steps page, but scoped to the Database Knowledge Graph chain. 1 User request Responsibility: Ingress payload reaches ConvEngine with user text, conversation id, and input params. Session Mutations: userText, inputParams, conversation bootstrap context. Config/Table Dependencies: HTTP request body, ce_conversation. } } The Database Knowledge Graph story starts before any classifier runs. The raw message is preserved as the exact text that later gets classified, planned, and audited. 2 IntentResolutionStep Responsibility: Classifies the turn intent before MCP planning begins. Session Mutations: intent, state hints, classifier diagnostics. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_intent_classifier, ce_intent, ce_config. } This decides whether the turn is greeting, normal FAQ, or Database Knowledge Graph diagnostics. In the Zapper path it resolves the investigative intent. 3 RulesStep Responsibility: Applies rule driven state transitions after intent is known. Session Mutations: state, derived input params, context flags. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_rule. } Rules push the session into an MCP friendly state like ANALYZE and can normalize values the planner will need later. 4 McpToolStep Responsibility: Starts the planner/tool loop for MCP execution. Session Mutations: context.mcp. , tool observations, planner state. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_mcp_tool, ce_mcp_planner, ce_mcp_db_tool. } This is the orchestration gate. It asks the planner what comes next, runs the tool, records the observation, and repeats until ANSWER. 5 McpPlanner Responsibility: Selects the next MCP action from configured planner context. Session Mutations: nextToolCode, planner rationale, finalAnswer draft. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_mcp_planner, planner prompt template. } For Database Knowledge Graph it chooses the strict sequence: resolve, lookup, plan, validate, execute, then ANSWER. 6 DbkgCaseResolveToolHandler Responsibility: Bridges the MCP tool call into the Database Knowledge Graph case resolver runtime. Session Mutations: Writes dbkg.case.resolve observation payload. Config/Table Dependencies: convengine.mcp.db.knowledge graph.caseResolveToolCode. } The handler is thin by design. It only adapts the MCP call into runtime service execution. 7 DbkgCaseResolver Responsibility: Scores the user question against DB driven case signals. Session Mutations: selectedCase, rankedCases. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_mcp_case_type, ce_mcp_case_signal. } This converts support language into a structured case family such as SERVICE_DISCONNECT. 8 DbkgKnowledgeLookupToolHandler Responsibility: Bridges the lookup tool call into the knowledge service. Session Mutations: Writes dbkg.knowledge.lookup observation payload. Config/Table Dependencies: convengine.mcp.db.knowledge graph.knowledgeLookupToolCode. } This remains an MCP facing adapter while the ranking work lives in the service. 9 DbkgKnowledgeLookupService Responsibility: Builds the ranked Database Knowledge Graph knowledge pack for the current case. Session Mutations: entities, systems, apiFlows, objects, columns, joins, statuses, lineage. Config/Table Dependencies: Semantic graph tables, JDBC schema metadata, overlay tables. } This combines live schema introspection with optional semantic overlays so Database Knowledge Graph can reason from both database structure and business metadata. 10 DbkgInvestigatePlanToolHandler Responsibility: Bridges the plan tool call into playbook resolution. Session Mutations: Writes dbkg.investigate.plan observation payload. Config/Table Dependencies: convengine.mcp.db.knowledge graph.investigatePlanToolCode. } The handler passes the investigative context to the playbook resolver so the engine can choose the exact diagnostic path. 11 DbkgPlaybookResolver Responsibility: Chooses the matching playbook and loads the step graph. Session Mutations: selectedPlaybook, rankedPlaybooks, steps, transitions. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_mcp_playbook, ce_mcp_playbook_signal, ce_mcp_playbook_step, ce_mcp_playbook_transition. } After the case is known, this narrows the problem to one executable playbook and materializes the DAG. 12 DbkgPlaybookValidateToolHandler Responsibility: Bridges the validation tool call into the graph validator. Session Mutations: Writes dbkg.playbook.validate observation payload. Config/Table Dependencies: convengine.mcp.db.knowledge graph.playbookValidateToolCode. } Validation becomes a first class MCP observation so the planner and guardrails can inspect it. 13 DbkgPlaybookValidator Responsibility: Validates the Database Knowledge Graph step DAG before SQL runs. Session Mutations: valid, canExecute, graphError, startStepCode. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_mcp_playbook_step, ce_mcp_playbook_transition. } It checks missing nodes, ambiguous starts, illegal transitions, unreachable steps, and explicit start markers. 14 DbkgInvestigateExecuteToolHandler Responsibility: Bridges the execute tool call into the playbook engine. Session Mutations: Writes dbkg.investigate.execute observation payload. Config/Table Dependencies: convengine.mcp.db.knowledge graph.investigateExecuteToolCode. } Execution only happens after validation. This handler keeps that final tool call clean. 15 DbkgPlaybookEngine Responsibility: Runs the validated playbook DAG and resolves the diagnosis. Session Mutations: runtime step outputs, query params, finalSummary, outcomeCode. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_mcp_query_template, ce_mcp_query_param_rule, ce_mcp_playbook_step, ce_mcp_playbook_transition, ce_mcp_outcome_rule. } This is the core Database Knowledge Graph runtime engine. It follows transitions, evaluates condition_expr, executes approved templates, detects cycles, and resolves the final outcome. 16 McpToolStep Responsibility: Re enters the planner loop after execution and enforces the validation guard. Session Mutations: new observation appended, guardrail status, planner context. Config/Table Dependencies: context.mcp.observations, ce_audit. } This second appearance matters: after execute finishes, McpToolStep feeds the new observation back to the planner and enforces the Database Knowledge Graph validation requirement. 17 McpPlanner Responsibility: Consumes the final Database Knowledge Graph observation and emits ANSWER. Session Mutations: finalAnswer, planner completion state. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_mcp_planner. } At this point the planner no longer chooses another tool. It converts the gathered observations into the ANSWER action. 18 RulesStep Responsibility: Applies POST_AGENT_MCP rule phase after planner ANSWER. Session Mutations: post mcp state transitions, final response flags. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_rule (POST_AGENT_MCP). } This is where consumers react to the MCP outcome using rule rows instead of hardcoded Java. 19 ResponseResolutionStep Responsibility: Builds the final assistant payload using the resolved response mapping. Session Mutations: payload, lastAssistantJson, finalResult. Config/Table Dependencies: ce_response, ce_prompt_template, ce_output_schema. } This turns the MCP answer into the actual assistant visible message shape and final payload contract. 20 Assistant output Responsibility: The user sees one final assistant response, not internal Database Knowledge Graph sub steps. Session Mutations: final HTTP response body only. Config/Table Dependencies: finalResult payload. } Even though Database Knowledge Graph runs several internal observations, ConvEngine still returns one assistant message. The operator sees the diagnosis, not the orchestration noise. Config surface Database Knowledge Graph metadata tables 1. Resolution + intent mapping 2. Business + system graph Central Order BillBank."] ]} / 3. Physical schema overlay + lookup aids 4. Execution + query graph Why Database Knowledge Graph is different from legacy DB tools Runtime code path (what, how, why) DbKnowledgeGraphRuntimeService This is the thin facade. It should stay small. It exposes the public Database Knowledge Graph entry points and delegates to focused services. public Map lookupKnowledge(Map args, EngineSession session) public Map planInvestigation(Map args, EngineSession session) public Map validatePlaybook(Map args, EngineSession session) public Map executeInvestigation(Map args, EngineSession session) } Why it exists: it keeps the MCP handlers stable while the implementation stays decomposed. DbkgPlaybookValidator This is the structural safety gate for the Database Knowledge Graph graph. ... if (explicitStarts.size() 1) ... if (!explicitStarts.isEmpty() && !startNodes.contains(explicitStarts.get(0))) ... return null; } } What it does: verifies every transition references a real step enforces a single start point rejects explicit starts that already have incoming edges finds unreachable nodes before execution time Why it matters: a DB driven DAG is only safe if the metadata graph is validated before it can run. DbkgPlaybookEngine This is the execution engine for the Database Knowledge Graph graph. String currentStepCode = playbookValidator.resolveStartStepCode(steps, transitions); while (currentStepCode != null && iterations step = stepsByCode.get(currentStepCode); Map stepResult = executeStep(step, runtime); stepResults.add(stepResult); support.stepOutputs(runtime).put(String.valueOf(step.get("stepCode")), stepResult.get("output")); currentStepCode = resolveNextStepCode(currentStepCode, stepResult, runtime, transitions); } } What it does: validates the graph initializes runtime context executes the current node through the step executor factory writes step outputs for downstream parameter reuse follows transition edges based on step status + condition_expr resolves final outcome from ce_mcp_outcome_rule Why it matters: execution order is now DB driven by ce_mcp_playbook_transition , not by Java if/else chains. DbkgOutcomeResolver This is the shared condition expression evaluator used by both transition routing and final outcomes. List orParts = splitByLogicalOperator(normalized, "OR"); if (orParts.size() 1) List andParts = splitByLogicalOperator(normalized, "AND"); if (andParts.size() 1) return evaluateAtomicCondition(normalized, variables); } } What it does: supports AND , OR , atomic comparisons, truthy checks, and top level parentheses reuses the same expression semantics in transitions and outcomes Why it matters: branching stays in DB metadata instead of becoming hardcoded Java branching logic. McpToolStep hard guard The planner prompt is not enough. Runtime still enforces validation before execution. if (!isAllowedByConfiguredNextToolGuardrail(nextToolCode, observations)) return null; } } What it does: dbkg.investigate.execute is blocked unless a prior dbkg.playbook.validate observation exists with valid=true and canExecute=true . Why it matters: even if the planner misorders tools, Database Knowledge Graph still refuses to run the playbook. McpSqlGuardrail This is the shared SQL safety gate used by both the generic MCP DB tool path and the Database Knowledge Graph query template executor. if (SELECT_INTO.matcher(normalized).find()) if (FOR_UPDATE.matcher(normalized).find()) ... if (!rules.allowedFunctions.isEmpty()) } } What it does: blocks write/admin statements such as INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE , DROP , TRUNCATE , ALTER , CREATE , MERGE , and CALL rejects multi statement SQL rejects SELECT INTO and FOR UPDATE loads extra rules from ce_mcp_sql_guardrail optionally enforces a function allowlist when ALLOW_FUNCTION rows exist Why it matters: read only is enforced in Java now, not just implied by convention or metadata. SQL trace observability Both SQL backed MCP paths now emit dedicated audit stages and optional ce_verbose hooks: MCP_DB_SQL_EXECUTION from McpDbExecutor for classic ce_mcp_db_tool SQL templates DBKG_QUERY_SQL_EXECUTION from DbkgQueryTemplateStepExecutor for Database Knowledge Graph query template steps Both payloads include: sql params row_count rows That gives consumers one place to inspect the executed SQL text and the returned row set, and one place to override the user facing progress text through ce_verbose . SQL package Recommended read path 1. Start with this page to understand the Database Knowledge Graph control model. 2. Load the Database Knowledge Graph SQL package ( src/main/resources/sql/mcp/dbkg ). 3. Load the Zapper demo schema ( src/main/resources/sql/zapper ). 4. Walk through the concrete trace in . For a real end to end trace with actual transaction tables, open . That page shows the exact chat turns, runtime tool sequence, DB tables touched, and why the engine produces each diagnosis.

It documents the new dbkg.* tool chain, the ce_* metadata package behind it, and the runtime safety model that makes the DB investigation flow configurable instead of hardcoded in Java. <Highlight type="info" title="Core idea"> dbkg.* is not a raw SQL generator. It is a DB-driven investigation pipeline: resolve the case, load graph knowledge, choose a playbook, validate the playbook graph, then execute approved query steps. </Highlight> ## Story first: how Database Knowledge Graph works from a real user question Start from the operator, not the table. A real user does not know zp_ui_data_history , ce_mcp_query_template , or ce_mcp_playbook_transition . They speak in support language, and the engine has to do the translation. The story below is intentionally broken into real turn-by-turn cards so you can see exactly what happens before, during, and after the Database Knowledge Graph tool chain. ### Turn 0 <Tabs groupId="dbkg-story-turn-0"> <TabItem value="conversation" label="User + Agent" default> <ChatContainer> <TraceChatBubble role="user" name="Turn 0 - User" message="Hi" info={[ "Simple greeting turn.", "This is the baseline path before any Database Knowledge Graph work starts." ]} /> <TraceChatBubble role="assistant" name="Turn 0 - Assistant" intent="GREETING" state="IDLE" message="Hi, how can I help you today?" json={{ intent: "GREETING", state: "IDLE", mcp: { toolChainSkipped: true } }} tables={[ "ce_intent_classifier (R)", "ce_response (R)", "ce_audit (W)" ]} info={[ "Intent classifier resolves GREETING.", "McpToolStep does not start the Database Knowledge Graph chain for this turn." ]} /> </ChatContainer> </TabItem> <TabItem value="inside" label="Inside Engine"> <DbTable title="Turn 0 internal path" columns={["Layer", "What happened", "Why"]} rows={[ ["IntentResolutionStep", "Matches GREETING.", "The turn is clearly conversational, not investigative."], ["McpToolStep", "Skipped for greeting.", "No planner/tool cost is paid for a greeting-only turn."], ["ResponseResolutionStep", "Uses the greeting response mapping.", "Returns one normal assistant response."] ]} /> </TabItem> <TabItem value="tables" label="Tables"> <DbTable title="Turn 0 tables" columns={["Table", "Access", "Purpose"]} rows={[ ["ce_intent_classifier", "R", "Greeting pattern match."], ["ce_response", "R", "Greeting response row."], ["ce_audit", "W", "Turn audit trail."] ]} /> </TabItem> <TabItem value="return" label="Engine Return"> <CodeBlockToggle title="Turn 0 engine result" language="json" defaultOpen={true}> { { "intent": "GREETING", "state": "IDLE", "mcp": { "toolChainSkipped": true }, "assistantMessage": "Hi, how can I help you today?" } } </CodeBlockToggle> </TabItem> </Tabs> ### Turn 1 <Tabs groupId="dbkg-story-turn-1"> <TabItem value="conversation" label="User + Agent" default> <ChatContainer> <TraceChatBubble role="user" name="Turn 1 - User" message="I need help with a Zapper disconnect issue for UPS in Irving." info={[ "This is the entry into the Database Knowledge Graph diagnostic path.", "The wording includes business terms, not technical table names." ]} /> <TraceChatBubble role="assistant" name="Turn 1 - Assistant" intent="DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS" state="ANALYZE" message="I can help investigate that. Tell me the exact symptom or any ids you have, such as request id, account id, accLocId, or disconnect id." json={{ intent: "DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS", state: "ANALYZE", schemaNeeded: ["knownIds", "customerName", "hours"] }} tables={[ "ce_intent_classifier (R)", "ce_rule (POST_AGENT_INTENT) (R)", "ce_output_schema (R)", "ce_response (R)", "ce_prompt_template (R)", "ce_audit (W)" ]} info={[ "Intent classifier routes the turn into DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS.", "ce_rule moves the flow into ANALYZE.", "ce_output_schema defines which identifiers are useful for the investigation." ]} /> </ChatContainer> </TabItem> <TabItem value="inside" label="Inside Engine"> <DbTable title="Turn 1 internal path" columns={["Layer", "What happened", "Why"]} rows={[ ["ce_intent_classifier", "Zapper/disconnect language matches DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS.", "This is what routes the user into the MCP diagnostic path."], ["ce_rule", "POST_AGENT_INTENT moves state to ANALYZE.", "The conversation is now in the state where MCP can investigate."], ["ce_output_schema", "Exposes useful fields like accountId, accLocId, and zpDisconnectId.", "The next turn can provide structured evidence."], ["ResponseResolutionStep", "Asks for the symptom/ids needed to continue.", "This is still a single normal assistant response, not a tool result dump."] ]} /> </TabItem> <TabItem value="tables" label="Tables"> <DbTable title="Turn 1 tables" columns={["Table", "Access", "Purpose"]} rows={[ ["ce_intent_classifier", "R", "Resolve DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS."], ["ce_rule", "R", "Move to ANALYZE state."], ["ce_output_schema", "R", "Define useful structured investigation fields."], ["ce_prompt_template", "R", "Shape the collection prompt."], ["ce_response", "R", "Return the request-for-details response."], ["ce_audit", "W", "Audit the turn."] ]} /> </TabItem> <TabItem value="return" label="Engine Return"> <CodeBlockToggle title="Turn 1 engine result" language="json" defaultOpen={true}> { { "intent": "DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS", "state": "ANALYZE", "schemaNeeded": ["knownIds", "customerName", "hours"], "assistantMessage": "I can help investigate that. Tell me the exact symptom or any ids you have ..." } } </CodeBlockToggle> </TabItem> </Tabs> ### Turn 2 <Tabs groupId="dbkg-story-turn-2"> <TabItem value="conversation" label="User + Agent" default> <ChatContainer> <TraceChatBubble role="user" name="Turn 2 - User" message="AccountId is UPSA100, accLocId is ACCLOC1001, and ASR says inventory not found for the Irving disconnect." info={[ "The user now provides concrete identifiers plus the actual symptom.", "This is the first point where the Database Knowledge Graph flow has enough grounded evidence to investigate." ]} /> <TraceChatBubble role="assistant" name="Turn 2 - Assistant" intent="DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS" state="COMPLETED" message="I found the request. The account-location mapping exists, but there is no matching inventory row for ACCLOC1001, so this looks like an inventory or provisioning gap rather than an ASR UI issue." json={{ mcp: { toolOrder: [ "dbkg.case.resolve", "dbkg.knowledge.lookup", "dbkg.investigate.plan", "dbkg.playbook.validate", "dbkg.investigate.execute" ], selectedCase: "INVENTORY_MISMATCH", selectedPlaybook: "ASR_INVENTORY_NOT_FOUND", validation: { valid: true, canExecute: true }, execution: { outcome: "INVENTORY_GAP", requestFound: true, inventoryFound: false } } }} tables={[ "ce_mcp_case_type (R)", "ce_mcp_playbook (R)", "zp_request (R)", "zp_account_location (R)", "zp_inventory_service (R)", "ce_mcp_outcome_rule (R)", "ce_response (R)", "ce_audit (W)" ]} info={[ "The Database Knowledge Graph chain now runs because the turn contains both identifiers and the symptom.", "The selected playbook is the inventory-mismatch playbook.", "The engine answers with a diagnosis, not just a raw query dump." ]} /> </ChatContainer> </TabItem> <TabItem value="inside" label="Inside Engine"> <DbTable title="Turn 2 internal path" columns={["Layer", "What happened", "Why"]} rows={[ ["dbkg.case.resolve", "Resolves INVENTORY_MISMATCH.", "The phrase 'inventory not found' is a strong case signal."], ["dbkg.knowledge.lookup", "Loads inventory lineage, account-location mappings, and relevant join hints.", "The engine now knows which tables represent the business story."], ["dbkg.investigate.plan", "Chooses ASR_INVENTORY_NOT_FOUND.", "That playbook matches the symptom and identifiers."], ["dbkg.investigate.execute", "Checks request existence, account-location mapping, then inventory presence.", "This is a multi-step diagnosis, not one guessed SQL statement."], ["ResponseResolutionStep", "Returns one normal assistant message from the MCP result.", "The user sees a single clear diagnosis."] ]} /> </TabItem> <TabItem value="tables" label="Tables"> <DbTable title="Turn 2 tables" columns={["Table", "Access", "Purpose"]} rows={[ ["ce_mcp_case_signal", "R", "Map the symptom to INVENTORY_MISMATCH."], ["ce_mcp_playbook", "R", "Select the inventory-mismatch playbook."], ["zp_request", "R", "Confirm the request exists."], ["zp_account_location", "R", "Resolve the account/location linkage."], ["zp_inventory_service", "R", "Check whether inventory exists for the linkage."], ["ce_mcp_outcome_rule", "R", "Classify the result as an inventory gap."], ["ce_audit", "W", "Persist the investigation trace."] ]} /> </TabItem> <TabItem value="return" label="Engine Return"> <CodeBlockToggle title="Turn 2 engine result" language="json" defaultOpen={true}> { { "intent": "DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS", "state": "COMPLETED", "mcp": { "selectedCase": "INVENTORY_MISMATCH", "selectedPlaybook": "ASR_INVENTORY_NOT_FOUND", "validation": { "valid": true, "canExecute": true }, "execution": { "outcome": "INVENTORY_GAP", "requestFound": true, "inventoryFound": false } }, "assistantMessage": "I found the request. The account-location mapping exists, but there is no matching inventory row ..." } } </CodeBlockToggle> </TabItem> </Tabs> ### Turn 2.1 <Tabs groupId="dbkg-story-turn-2-1"> <TabItem value="conversation" label="User + Agent" default> <ChatContainer> <TraceChatBubble role="user" name="Turn 2.1 - Resolution + intent mapping <DbTable title="Case and playbook resolution tables" columns={["Table", "Purpose", "How runtime uses it"]} rows={[ ["ce_mcp_case_type", "Defines top-level investigation families such as SERVICE_DISCONNECT or INVENTORY_MISMATCH.", "DbkgCaseResolver loads candidates from here before any ranking happens."], ["ce_mcp_case_signal", "Weighted signal rows (keyword/entity/status) that point to a case.", "User question tokens are scored here to decide which case best matches the request."], ["ce_mcp_playbook", "Defines executable diagnostic playbooks under each case.", "DbkgPlaybookResolver picks one playbook after the case is resolved."], ["ce_mcp_playbook_signal", "Weighted signals that point to a playbook under a case.", "Gives more precise routing than case-level matching once the case family is known."] ]} /> ### 2. Business + system graph <DbTable title="Semantic graph tables" columns={["Table", "Purpose", "How runtime uses it"]} rows={[ ["ce_mcp_domain_entity", "Business nouns such as Customer, Disconnect Request, Billing Record, with synonyms + llm_hint + metadata_json.", "Returned by dbkg.knowledge.lookup so the planner can reason in business language instead of table names only."], ["ce_mcp_domain_relation", "Edges between domain entities.", "Documents the business graph and can be surfaced in ranked knowledge results."], ["ce_mcp_system_node", "System registry such as ZapperCentral, Zapper Order Service, BillBank.", "Used to explain which application layer owns the failure or state transition."], ["ce_mcp_api_flow", "Declarative call-chain metadata with involved tables/IDs in metadata_json.", "Lets Database Knowledge Graph tell the planner which APIs and downstream systems are relevant to the question."], ["ce_mcp_system_relation", "Edges between systems.", "Documents orchestration order such as UI -> Central -> Order -> BillBank."] ]} /> ### 3. Physical schema overlay + lookup aids <DbTable title="Schema overlay tables" columns={["Table", "Purpose", "How runtime uses it"]} rows={[ ["ce_mcp_db_object", "Optional overlay for tables/views discovered from JDBC metadata.", "Adds business mapping, llm_hint, or metadata_json on top of auto-discovered objects."], ["ce_mcp_db_column", "Optional overlay for physical columns.", "Adds semantic names, synonyms, and hints without forcing consumers to hand-maintain every column."], ["ce_mcp_db_join_path", "Optional preferred join definitions.", "Overrides or supplements foreign-key-derived joins when business-preferred joins differ from raw schema edges."], ["ce_mcp_status_dictionary", "Maps status code fields to business labels (for example 200=ASSIGNED ).", "Used by param resolution and by the planner to convert business words into executable SQL filters."], ["ce_mcp_id_lineage", "Cross-system ID propagation metadata.", "Explains how request ids, order ids, disconnect ids, and billing ids line up across systems."] ]} /> ### 4. Execution + query graph <DbTable title="Execution graph tables" columns={["Table", "Purpose", "How runtime uses it"]} rows={[ ["ce_mcp_executor_template", "Registry of generic executor types (SQL, DERIVED_FIELD, SUMMARY, DECISION).", "Lets playbook steps point to behavior by code instead of Java-specific branching."], ["ce_mcp_query_template", "Approved SQL templates with purpose, contracts, limits, and safety metadata.", "DbkgQueryTemplateStepExecutor runs only these templates, not arbitrary free-form SQL."], ["ce_mcp_query_param_rule", "Rules for how each template parameter is filled.", "Values can come from args, case context, derived context, prior step output, or status dictionaries."], ["ce_mcp_playbook_step", "Playbook nodes.", "Each row is a runtime node with executor, config_json, and optional explicit start marker."], ["ce_mcp_playbook_transition", "Playbook edges.", "This is the runtime DAG; transitions are selected by outcome code plus condition_expr."], ["ce_mcp_outcome_rule", "Final diagnosis rules.", "After execution, the engine resolves the final explanation and next action from these conditions."] ]} /> ## Why Database Knowledge Graph is different from legacy DB tools <DbTable title="Semantic catalog (db.semantic.catalog) vs Database Knowledge Graph" columns={["Concern", "Semantic catalog (db.semantic.catalog)", "Database Knowledge Graph"]} rows={[ ["Primary job", "Rank SQL and schema hints.", "Resolve a business case, select a playbook, then execute a validated investigation graph."], ["Schema awareness", "Catalog-driven only.", "JDBC schema introspection + optional semantic overlays."], ["Workflow model", "Flat lookup.", "Graph-driven playbooks with explicit transitions and conditions."], ["Safety", "Prompt behavior only.", "Planner validation step + hard runtime guard in McpToolStep before execute."], ["Output", "Query-oriented hint.", "Diagnosis-oriented explanation tied to evidence and playbook outcomes."] ]} /> ## Runtime code path (what, how, why) ### DbKnowledgeGraphRuntimeService This is the thin facade. It should stay small. It exposes the public Database Knowledge Graph entry points and delegates to focused services. <CodeBlockToggle title="DbKnowledgeGraphRuntimeService (facade)" language="java" filePath="src/main/java/com/github/salilvnair/convengine/engine/mcp/knowledge/DbKnowledgeGraphRuntimeService.java" defaultOpen={true}> { public Map<String, Object> resolveCase(Map<String, Object> args, EngineSession session) { return caseResolver.resolveCase(args, session); } public Map<String, Object> lookupKnowledge(Map<String, Object> args, EngineSession session) { return knowledgeLookupService.lookupKnowledge(args, session); } public Map<String, Object> planInvestigation(Map<String, Object> args, EngineSession session) { return playbookResolver.planInvestigation(args, session); } public Map<String, Object> validatePlaybook(Map<String, Object> args, EngineSession session) { Map<String, Object> plan = playbookResolver.planInvestigation(args, session); ... } public Map<String, Object> executeInvestigation(Map<String, Object> args, EngineSession session) { return playbookEngine.executeInvestigation(args, session); } } </CodeBlockToggle> Why it exists: it keeps the MCP handlers stable while the implementation stays decomposed. ### DbkgPlaybookValidator This is the structural safety gate for the Database Knowledge Graph graph. <CodeBlockToggle title="DbkgPlaybookValidator.validateGraph" language="java" filePath="src/main/java/com/github/salilvnair/convengine/engine/mcp/knowledge/DbkgPlaybookValidator.java"> { public String validateGraph(List<Map<String, Object>> steps, List<Map<String, Object>> transitions) { if (steps.isEmpty()) { return "No steps defined."; } ... if (explicitStarts.size() > 1) { return "Multiple explicit start steps found"; } ... if (!explicitStarts.isEmpty() && !startNodes.contains(explicitStarts.get(0))) { return "Explicit start step must be a graph root"; } ... return null; } } </CodeBlockToggle> What it does: - verifies every transition references a real step - enforces a single start point - rejects explicit starts that already have incoming edges - finds unreachable nodes before execution time Why it matters: a DB-driven DAG is only safe if the metadata graph is validated before it can run. ### DbkgPlaybookEngine This is the execution engine for the Database Knowledge Graph graph. <CodeBlockToggle title="DbkgPlaybookEngine.executeInvestigation" language="java" filePath="src/main/java/com/github/salilvnair/convengine/engine/mcp/knowledge/DbkgPlaybookEngine.java"> { String graphValidationError = playbookValidator.validateGraph(steps, transitions); if (graphValidationError != null) { ... } String currentStepCode = playbookValidator.resolveStartStepCode(steps, transitions); while (currentStepCode != null && iterations < maxIterations) { Map<String, Object> step = stepsByCode.get(currentStepCode); Map<String, Object> stepResult = executeStep(step, runtime); stepResults.add(stepResult); support.stepOutputs(runtime).put(String.valueOf(step.get("stepCode")), stepResult.get("output")); currentStepCode = resolveNextStepCode(currentStepCode, stepResult, runtime, transitions); } } </CodeBlockToggle> What it does: - validates the graph - initializes runtime context - executes the current node through the step-executor factory - writes step outputs for downstream parameter reuse - follows transition edges based on step status + condition_expr - resolves final outcome from ce_mcp_outcome_rule Why it matters: execution order is now DB-driven by ce_mcp_playbook_transition , not by Java if/else chains. ### DbkgOutcomeResolver This is the shared condition-expression evaluator used by both transition routing and final outcomes. <CodeBlockToggle title="DbkgOutcomeResolver.evaluateCondition" language="java" filePath="src/main/java/com/github/salilvnair/convengine/engine/mcp/knowledge/DbkgOutcomeResolver.java"> { public boolean evaluateCondition(String expr, Map<String, Object> variables) { String normalized = unwrapOuterParentheses(support.asText(expr).trim()); if (normalized.isEmpty() || "true".equalsIgnoreCase(normalized)) { return true; } List<String> orParts = splitByLogicalOperator(normalized, "OR"); if (orParts.size() > 1) { ... } List<String> andParts = splitByLogicalOperator(normalized, "AND"); if (andParts.size() > 1) { ... } return evaluateAtomicCondition(normalized, variables); } } </CodeBlockToggle> What it does: - supports AND , OR , atomic comparisons, truthy checks, and top-level parentheses - reuses the same expression semantics in transitions and outcomes Why it matters: branching stays in DB metadata instead of becoming hardcoded Java branching logic. ### McpToolStep hard guard The planner prompt is not enough. Runtime still enforces validation before execution. <CodeBlockToggle title="McpToolStep Database Knowledge Graph execution guard" language="java" filePath="src/main/java/com/github/salilvnair/convengine/engine/steps/McpToolStep.java"> { private String nextToolGuardrailBlockReason(String nextToolCode, List<McpObservation> observations) { if (!isAllowedByDbkgValidationGuard(nextToolCode, observations)) { return "DBKG_VALIDATION_REQUIRED_BEFORE_EXECUTION"; } if (!isAllowedByConfiguredNextToolGuardrail(nextToolCode, observations)) { return "NEXT_TOOL_GUARDRAIL_BLOCKED"; } return null; } } </CodeBlockToggle> What it does: dbkg.investigate.execute is blocked unless a prior dbkg.playbook.validate observation exists with valid=true and canExecute=true . Why it matters: even if the planner misorders tools, Database Knowledge Graph still refuses to run the playbook. ### McpSqlGuardrail This is the shared SQL safety gate used by both the generic MCP DB tool path and the Database Knowledge Graph query-template executor. <CodeBlockToggle title="McpSqlGuardrail.assertReadOnly" language="java" filePath="src/main/java/com/github/salilvnair/convengine/engine/mcp/McpSqlGuardrail.java"> { public void assertReadOnly(String sql, String source) { String normalized = normalize(sql); ... if (!(normalized.startsWith("select ") || normalized.startsWith("with "))) { throw new IllegalArgumentException(source + " SQL must begin with SELECT or WITH for read-only execution."); } if (SELECT_INTO.matcher(normalized).find()) { throw new IllegalArgumentException(source + " SQL uses SELECT INTO, which is not allowed in read-only mode."); } if (FOR_UPDATE.matcher(normalized).find()) { throw new IllegalArgumentException(source + " SQL uses FOR UPDATE, which is not allowed in read-only mode."); } ... if (!rules.allowedFunctions.isEmpty()) { ... } } } </CodeBlockToggle> What it does: - blocks write/admin statements such as INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE , DROP , TRUNCATE , ALTER , CREATE , MERGE , and CALL - rejects multi-statement SQL - rejects SELECT INTO and FOR UPDATE - loads extra rules from ce_mcp_sql_guardrail - optionally enforces a function allowlist when ALLOW_FUNCTION rows exist Why it matters: read-only is enforced in Java now, not just implied by convention or metadata. ### SQL trace observability Both SQL-backed MCP paths now emit dedicated audit stages and optional ce_verbose hooks: - MCP_DB_SQL_EXECUTION from McpDbExecutor for classic ce_mcp_db_tool SQL templates - DBKG_QUERY_SQL_EXECUTION from DbkgQueryTemplateStepExecutor for Database Knowledge Graph query-template steps Both payloads include: - sql - params - row_count - rows That gives consumers one place to inspect the executed SQL text and the returned row set, and one place to override the user-facing progress text through ce_verbose . <DbTable title="ce_mcp_sql_guardrail rule types" columns={["rule_type", "match_value example", "What runtime does"]} rows={[ ["ALLOW_FUNCTION", "date_part", "Adds a function to the allowed function list on top of the built-in safe defaults."], ["BLOCK_FUNCTION", "pg_sleep", "Always blocks that SQL function if detected in a query."], ["BLOCK_KEYWORD", "explain", "Extends the blocked SQL token set beyond the built-in destructive/admin keywords."] ]} /> ## SQL package <Tabs groupId="dbkg-sql-tabs"> <TabItem value="ddl" label="DDL" default> <CodeBlockToggle title="Database Knowledge Graph DDL excerpt" language="sql" filePath="src/main/resources/sql/mcp/dbkg/ddl_postgres.sql"> { CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_case_type (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_case_signal (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_playbook (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_playbook_signal (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_domain_entity (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_domain_relation (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_system_node (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_api_flow (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_system_relation (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_db_object (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_db_column (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_db_join_path (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_status_dictionary (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_id_lineage (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_executor_template (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_query_template (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_query_param_rule (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_playbook_step (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_playbook_transition (...); CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_outcome_rule (...); } </CodeBlockToggle> </TabItem> <TabItem value="seed" label="Seed SQL"> <CodeBlockToggle title="Database Knowledge Graph seed excerpt" language="sql" filePath="src/main/resources/sql/mcp/dbkg/seed.sql"> { INSERT INTO ce_mcp_tool (...) VALUES (9101, 'dbkg.case.resolve', ...), (9102, 'dbkg.knowledge.lookup', ...), (9103, 'dbkg.investigate.plan', ...), (9104, 'dbkg.investigate.execute', ...), (9105, 'dbkg.playbook.validate', ...); INSERT INTO ce_mcp_planner (...) VALUES (5901, 'DBKG_DIAGNOSTICS', 'ANALYZE', ...); INSERT INTO ce_mcp_query_template (...); INSERT INTO ce_mcp_playbook_step (...); INSERT INTO ce_mcp_playbook_transition (...); INSERT INTO ce_mcp_outcome_rule (...); } </CodeBlockToggle> </TabItem> </Tabs> ## Recommended read path 1. Start with this page to understand the Database Knowledge Graph control model. 2. Load the Database Knowledge Graph SQL package ( src/main/resources/sql/mcp/dbkg ). 3. Load the Zapper demo schema ( src/main/resources/sql/zapper ). 4. Walk through the concrete trace in [MCP Example 4 - Zapper Disconnect](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example4). <Highlight type="success" title="Next step"> For a real end-to-end trace with actual transaction tables, open [MCP Example 4 - Zapper Disconnect](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example4). That page shows the exact chat turns, runtime tool sequence, DB tables touched, and why the engine produces each diagnosis. </Highlight>

title: semantic catalog
content: MCP Semantic Catalog ( db.semantic.catalog ) This page documents the semantic DB metadata lookup path used by MCP when tool code is db.semantic.catalog . db.semantic.catalog is a ranking and grounding tool. It returns schema/query hints from metadata tables. It does not execute business SQL. Where it fits db.semantic.catalog sits between free form user language and executable SQL tools such as postgres.query . 1. Planner receives user request and current MCP observations. 2. Planner calls db.semantic.catalog with a text question. 3. Semantic catalog returns ranked query + schema knowledge. 4. Planner uses those hints to decide next tool call or answer. 5. If SQL is needed, planner calls postgres.query separately. Runtime components Tool contract json } Accepted argument aliases: question (preferred) query user_input json ], "schemaKnowledge": [ ], "dbkgCapsule": , "sqlGraph": , "validValuesByObject": } } }, "insights": } Ranking model DbSemanticCatalogService ranking behavior is deterministic: 1. tokenize user question with lowercase normalization 2. split by non alphanumeric separators 3. remove stop words and tiny tokens 4. build candidate document tokens from row text fields 5. compute overlap based score using harmonic mean of precision + recall 6. filter by min score 7. return top max results Quality is mostly driven by good tags , description , and realistic prepared_sql examples in knowledge tables. Capsule and ranking behavior When knowledge capsule=true , semantic catalog returns dbkgCapsule directly from semantic metadata tables. Planner consumes this capsule as primary grounding context ( } ). This avoids relying only on tiny ranked schemaKnowledge subsets when your semantic catalog is large. Ranked lists still exist ( queryKnowledge , schemaKnowledge ) for next step guidance. If vector search.enabled=true , ranking can run via interceptor path: framework default: embedding + Java cosine fallback consumer override: DB native vector search (for example pgvector cosine distance) Metadata tables used Config surface yaml convengine: mcp: db: semantic catalog: enabled: true knowledge capsule: true schema knowledge: true query knowledge: true max results: 5 scan limit: 5000 min score: 0.15 query catalog table: ce_mcp_query_knowledge query text column: query_text query description column: description prepared sql column: prepared_sql tags column: tags api hints column: api_hints schema catalog table: ce_mcp_schema_knowledge schema table name column: table_name schema column name column: column_name schema valid values column: valid_values schema description column: description schema tags column: tags vector search: enabled: false max results: 10 vector column: vector db.semantic.catalog is fixed in the handler and registered through ce_mcp_tool rows. There is no semantic catalog.tool code property. When query knowledge=false , the runtime skips ce_mcp_query_knowledge . When schema knowledge=false , it skips ce_mcp_schema_knowledge . When knowledge capsule=false , planner capsule extraction/build is skipped. Registration and seeding ce_mcp_tool must contain a row for semantic catalog: sql INSERT INTO ce_mcp_tool (tool_code, tool_group, intent_code, state_code, description, enabled) VALUES ('db.semantic.catalog', 'DB', 'ANY', 'ANY', 'Semantic query/schema knowledge retrieval', true); ce_mcp_db_tool is not required for this tool because execution is handler based ( DbSemanticCatalogToolHandler ), not SQL template based. Recommended ce_mcp_schema_knowledge shape: sql CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_schema_knowledge ( id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY, table_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, column_name VARCHAR(255), valid_values VARCHAR(2000), vector VECTOR, consumer managed (or TEXT if you store serialized vectors) description VARCHAR(2000), tags VARCHAR(1000) ); Consumer vector interceptor example java @Component @Order(10) // higher priority than framework fallback @RequiredArgsConstructor public class PgVectorSemanticSearchInterceptor implements SemanticCatalogVectorSearchInterceptor @Override public List rank( String sourceType, String question, List rows, ConvEngineMcpConfig.Db.Knowledge cfg) private String toPgVectorLiteral(float[] vector) sb.append("]"); return sb.toString(); } } Postgres query interceptor (execution safety) postgres.query now supports consumer overridable SQL interceptors before read only guardrail + execution. Runtime shape: 1. Planner generates SQL. 2. PostgresQueryToolHandler runs interceptor chain ( PostgresQueryInterceptor beans by order). 3. Normalized SQL is validated by McpSqlGuardrail . 4. Query executes. Framework default interceptor ( DefaultPostgresQueryInterceptor , lowest precedence) auto fixes a common LLM error: bad: to_timestamp(ts_col/1000.0) when ts_col is already timestamp/date fix: rewrites to raw ts_col Column type is resolved from information_schema.columns under current schemas, so this is schema aware and avoids runtime errors like: operator does not exist: timestamp with time zone / numeric Consumer override example: java @Component @Order(10) // higher priority than framework fallback public class MyPostgresQueryInterceptor implements PostgresQueryInterceptor } Safety model 1. Metadata table and column identifiers are validated against a strict safe identifier regex. 2. Service only performs table reads from configured knowledge catalogs. 3. No DML/DDL execution path exists in semantic catalog handler. 4. Executable SQL is delegated to separate tool ( postgres.query ) with read only guardrails. Semantic Catalog vs DBKG Audit and trace stages to watch MCP_PLAN_LLM_INPUT MCP_PLAN_LLM_OUTPUT MCP_TOOL_CALL ( tool_code=db.semantic.catalog ) MCP_TOOL_RESULT MCP_FINAL_ANSWER (or next tool call) If planner continues to postgres.query , also monitor: DYNAMIC_SQL_EXECUTION DYNAMIC_SQL_EXECUTION_ERROR Recommended read order 1. 2. 3. Semantic Catalog (this page) 4. 5.
rawContent: # MCP Semantic Catalog ( db.semantic.catalog ) This page documents the semantic DB metadata lookup path used by MCP when tool code is db.semantic.catalog . <Highlight type="info" title="What this tool is"> db.semantic.catalog is a ranking and grounding tool. It returns schema/query hints from metadata tables. It does not execute business SQL. </Highlight> ## Where it fits db.semantic.catalog sits between free-form user language and executable SQL tools such as postgres.query . 1. Planner receives user request and current MCP observations. 2. Planner calls db.semantic.catalog with a text question. 3. Semantic catalog returns ranked query + schema knowledge. 4. Planner uses those hints to decide next tool call or answer. 5. If SQL is needed, planner calls postgres.query separately. ## Runtime components <DbTable title="Semantic catalog component map" columns={["Component", "Type", "Role"]} rows={[ [" DbSemanticCatalogToolHandler ", "DbToolHandler", "MCP DB tool handler for db.semantic.catalog ; extracts question / query / user_input and delegates to service."], [" DbSemanticCatalogService ", "Service", "Loads metadata rows, builds capsule metadata, computes lexical/vector ranking, and returns compact JSON payload."], [" SemanticCatalogVectorSearchInterceptor ", "SPI", "Pluggable vector search strategy. Consumer bean can override framework fallback and use DB-native cosine/pgvector queries."], [" DefaultSemanticCatalogVectorSearchInterceptor ", "Service", "Least-priority framework fallback using LlmClient.generateEmbedding(...) + stored row vectors + cosine in Java."], [" McpDbToolExecutor ", "Executor", "Routes MCP DB tools to registered handlers first; falls back to ce_mcp_db_tool only when no handler matches."], [" McpPlanner ", "Planner", "Consumes semantic output inside mcp_observations , then picks next CALL_TOOL / ANSWER ."], [" ce_mcp_tool ", "Table", "Registers db.semantic.catalog as an MCP tool available in planner scope."], [" ce_mcp_query_knowledge ", "Table", "Query-level semantic hints and prepared SQL examples."], [" ce_mcp_schema_knowledge ", "Table", "Table/column semantic hints and tags."], ]} /> ## Tool contract <Tabs groupId="semantic-catalog-contract"> <TabItem value="request" label="Request" default> json { "action": "CALL_TOOL", "tool_code": "db.semantic.catalog", "args": { "question": "Find rejected requests from yesterday" } } Accepted argument aliases: - question (preferred) - query - user_input </TabItem> <TabItem value="response" label="Response"> json { "question": "Find rejected requests from yesterday", "queryKnowledge": [ { "score": 0.421, "queryText": "Find Rejected Requests recently", "description": "Finds requests transitioned from assigned to rejected.", "preparedSql": "SELECT ...", "tags": ["rejected", "history"], "apiHints": [] } ], "schemaKnowledge": [ { "score": 0.267, "tableName": "zp_ui_data_history", "columnName": "zp_action_id", "description": "Action/state transition code", "tags": ["state", "history"], "validValues": ["100", "200", "300", "400", "500"] } ], "dbkgCapsule": { "version": "semantic-catalog-capsule-v2", "sourceCoverage": { "ce_mcp_query_knowledge": 42, "ce_mcp_schema_knowledge": 380 }, "sqlGraph": { "objects": ["zp_request", "zp_billbank_record"], "columnsByObject": { "zp_request": ["zp_request_id", "request_status"], "zp_billbank_record": ["bill_cease_status", "record_status"] }, "validValuesByObject": { "zp_billbank_record": { "bill_cease_status": ["ACTIVE", "CEASED"] } } } }, "insights": { "suggestedPreparedQueries": ["SELECT ..."], "suggestedTables": ["zp_ui_data_history"] } } </TabItem> </Tabs> ## Ranking model DbSemanticCatalogService ranking behavior is deterministic: 1. tokenize user question with lowercase normalization 2. split by non-alphanumeric separators 3. remove stop words and tiny tokens 4. build candidate document tokens from row text fields 5. compute overlap-based score using harmonic mean of precision + recall 6. filter by min-score 7. return top max-results <Highlight type="tip" title="Practical implication"> Quality is mostly driven by good tags , description , and realistic prepared_sql examples in knowledge tables. </Highlight> ## Capsule and ranking behavior When knowledge-capsule=true , semantic catalog returns dbkgCapsule directly from semantic metadata tables. - Planner consumes this capsule as primary grounding context ( {{dbkg_capsule}} ). - This avoids relying only on tiny ranked schemaKnowledge subsets when your semantic catalog is large. - Ranked lists still exist ( queryKnowledge , schemaKnowledge ) for next-step guidance. If vector-search.enabled=true , ranking can run via interceptor path: - framework default: embedding + Java cosine fallback - consumer override: DB-native vector search (for example pgvector cosine distance) ## Metadata tables used <DbTable title=" ce_mcp_query_knowledge columns used" columns={["Column", "Used for", "Notes"]} rows={[ [" query_text ", "Ranking text", "Short searchable query intent."], [" description ", "Ranking text", "Natural language explanation for retrieval quality."], [" prepared_sql ", "Planner hint", "Candidate SQL template/hint shown to planner."], [" tags ", "Ranking text", "Comma/pipe delimited tag set."], [" api_hints ", "Ranking text", "Optional API/system hint tags."], ]} /> <DbTable title=" ce_mcp_schema_knowledge columns used" columns={["Column", "Used for", "Notes"]} rows={[ [" table_name ", "Ranking text + insight", "Primary table grounding for planner."], [" column_name ", "Ranking text", "Column-level grounding."], [" valid_values ", "Ranking text + planner hint", "Optional enum-like business values for states/codes/channels/types."], [" vector ", "Optional vector ranking", "Consumer-populated embedding vector used when vector-search is enabled."], [" description ", "Ranking text", "Business meaning for table/column."], [" tags ", "Ranking text", "Domain tags to improve retrieval."], ]} /> ## Config surface yaml convengine: mcp: db: semantic-catalog: enabled: true knowledge-capsule: true schema-knowledge: true query-knowledge: true max-results: 5 scan-limit: 5000 min-score: 0.15 query-catalog-table: ce_mcp_query_knowledge query-text-column: query_text query-description-column: description prepared-sql-column: prepared_sql tags-column: tags api-hints-column: api_hints schema-catalog-table: ce_mcp_schema_knowledge schema-table-name-column: table_name schema-column-name-column: column_name schema-valid-values-column: valid_values schema-description-column: description schema-tags-column: tags vector-search: enabled: false max-results: 10 vector-column: vector <Highlight type="info" title="No semantic tool-code property"> db.semantic.catalog is fixed in the handler and registered through ce_mcp_tool rows. There is no semantic-catalog.tool-code property. </Highlight> <Highlight type="tip" title="Selective source toggles"> When query-knowledge=false , the runtime skips ce_mcp_query_knowledge . When schema-knowledge=false , it skips ce_mcp_schema_knowledge . When knowledge-capsule=false , planner capsule extraction/build is skipped. </Highlight> ## Registration and seeding ce_mcp_tool must contain a row for semantic catalog: sql INSERT INTO ce_mcp_tool (tool_code, tool_group, intent_code, state_code, description, enabled) VALUES ('db.semantic.catalog', 'DB', 'ANY', 'ANY', 'Semantic query/schema knowledge retrieval', true); ce_mcp_db_tool is not required for this tool because execution is handler-based ( DbSemanticCatalogToolHandler ), not SQL-template based. Recommended ce_mcp_schema_knowledge shape: sql CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ce_mcp_schema_knowledge ( id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY, table_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, column_name VARCHAR(255), valid_values VARCHAR(2000), vector VECTOR, -- consumer-managed (or TEXT if you store serialized vectors) description VARCHAR(2000), tags VARCHAR(1000) ); ## Consumer vector interceptor example java @Component @Order(10) // higher priority than framework fallback @RequiredArgsConstructor public class PgVectorSemanticSearchInterceptor implements SemanticCatalogVectorSearchInterceptor { private final JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate; private final LlmClient llmClient; @Override public boolean supports(ConvEngineMcpConfig.Db.Knowledge.VectorSearch config) { return config != null && config.isEnabled(); } @Override public List<Map<String, Object>> rank( String sourceType, String question, List<Map<String, Object>> rows, ConvEngineMcpConfig.Db.Knowledge cfg) { float[] embedding = llmClient.generateEmbedding(question); String embeddingLiteral = toPgVectorLiteral(embedding); String table = "query".equalsIgnoreCase(sourceType) ? cfg.getQueryCatalogTable() : cfg.getSchemaCatalogTable(); int maxResults = Math.max(1, cfg.getVectorSearch().getMaxResults()); String vectorCol = cfg.getVectorSearch().getVectorColumn(); String sql = """ SELECT t.*, (1 - (t.%s <=> ?::vector)) AS _vector_score FROM %s t WHERE t.%s IS NOT NULL ORDER BY t.%s <=> ?::vector LIMIT ? """.formatted(vectorCol, table, vectorCol, vectorCol); return jdbcTemplate.queryForList(sql, embeddingLiteral, embeddingLiteral, maxResults); } private String toPgVectorLiteral(float[] vector) { StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("["); for (int i = 0; i < vector.length; i++) { if (i > 0) sb.append(","); sb.append(vector[i]); } sb.append("]"); return sb.toString(); } } ## Postgres query interceptor (execution safety) postgres.query now supports consumer-overridable SQL interceptors before read-only guardrail + execution. Runtime shape: 1. Planner generates SQL. 2. PostgresQueryToolHandler runs interceptor chain ( PostgresQueryInterceptor beans by order). 3. Normalized SQL is validated by McpSqlGuardrail . 4. Query executes. Framework default interceptor ( DefaultPostgresQueryInterceptor , lowest precedence) auto-fixes a common LLM error: - bad: to_timestamp(ts_col/1000.0) when ts_col is already timestamp/date - fix: rewrites to raw ts_col Column type is resolved from information_schema.columns under current schemas, so this is schema-aware and avoids runtime errors like: - operator does not exist: timestamp with time zone / numeric Consumer override example: java @Component @Order(10) // higher priority than framework fallback public class MyPostgresQueryInterceptor implements PostgresQueryInterceptor { @Override public String intercept(String sql, CeMcpTool tool, EngineSession session, Map<String, Object> args) { // enforce tenant/schema rules, sql normalization, org conventions, etc. return sql; } } ## Safety model 1. Metadata table and column identifiers are validated against a strict safe identifier regex. 2. Service only performs table reads from configured knowledge catalogs. 3. No DML/DDL execution path exists in semantic catalog handler. 4. Executable SQL is delegated to separate tool ( postgres.query ) with read-only guardrails. ## Semantic Catalog vs DBKG <DbTable title="Semantic Catalog vs Database Knowledge Graph (DBKG)" columns={["Concern", "Semantic Catalog ( db.semantic.catalog )", "DBKG ( dbkg.* )"]} rows={[ ["Primary purpose", "Rank metadata hints for planner grounding.", "Run full investigation graph (resolve, plan, validate, execute)."], ["Data model", "2 core knowledge tables.", "Large graph of case, entity, system, playbook, transitions, templates."], ["Execution", "No SQL execution.", "Can execute approved query templates after validation."], ["Operational cost", "Low and fast.", "Higher but richer diagnostic capability."], ["Best use", "Dynamic SQL grounding and schema hinting.", "Production-grade diagnosis workflow with explainable outcomes."], ]} /> ## Audit and trace stages to watch - MCP_PLAN_LLM_INPUT - MCP_PLAN_LLM_OUTPUT - MCP_TOOL_CALL ( tool_code=db.semantic.catalog ) - MCP_TOOL_RESULT - MCP_FINAL_ANSWER (or next tool call) If planner continues to postgres.query , also monitor: - DYNAMIC_SQL_EXECUTION - DYNAMIC_SQL_EXECUTION_ERROR ## Recommended read order 1. [MCP Basics](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/basics) 2. [Advanced Guide](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/advanced) 3. Semantic Catalog (this page) 4. [DB Knowledge Graph](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/knowledge) 5. [Example 4 - Zapper Disconnect](/docs/v2/deep-dive/mcp/example4)

title: semantic query
content: }, }, }, }, }, }, }, }, }, }, }, }, } ]; , , , , , , , , , , , ]; u: , m: , h: , rts: , s1: , s2: , s3: , s4: , s5: , s6: , s7: , pa: , o: }; MCP Semantic Query ( db.semantic.query ) This page documents the semantic query engine under: com.github.salilvnair.convengine.engine.mcp.query.semantic It is the Text to AST to SQL runtime for ConvEngine MCP mode semantic . Semantic Query generates AST JSON with LLM, compiles SQL deterministically in Java, enforces policy guardrails, executes read only SQL, and returns auditable result summaries. Runtime request flow semantic stages planner answer" nodes= edges= detailsById= defaultSelectedId="rts" / Stage pipeline (actual order) Package Responsibilities canonical mapping", "SemanticQueryAstV1, AstFilterGroup, DefaultAstValidator, V1CanonicalAstMapper"], ["retrieval/ ", "Entity/table retrieval models + retrievers + vector adapters", "CandidateEntity, RetrievalResult, DefaultSemanticEntityTableRetriever, PostgresPgVectorSearchAdapter"], ["graph/ ", "Schema graph + join path planning contracts/providers", "SchemaGraphEngine, JoinPathResolver, JGraphTSchemaGraphEngine, DefaultJoinPathResolver"], ["llm/ ", "LLM AST JSON generation", "DefaultSemanticAstGenerator"], ["sql/ ", "AST SQL compile core + clause/operator/window handlers + policy", "DefaultSemanticSqlCompiler, DefaultAstQueryAssemblyClauseHandler, DefaultInAstOperatorHandler"], ["execute/ ", "Compiled SQL execution", "DefaultSemanticSqlExecutor"], ["summary/ ", "Result formatting", "DefaultSemanticResultSummarizer"], ["runtime/ ", "MCP tool entry + pipeline + stage orchestration", "DbSemanticQueryToolHandler, SemanticQueryRuntimeService, SemanticStagePipelineFactory"], ["model/ ", "Semantic model loading and registry", "SemanticModelLoader, SemanticModelRegistry"] ]} / Libraries we use Semantic Query runtime is built with a small set of focused libraries in convengine/pom.xml . Core dependencies (actual) xml org.jooq jooq org.jgrapht jgrapht core 1.5.2 com.fasterxml.jackson.dataformat jackson dataformat yaml jOOQ in Semantic Query jOOQ is not used as a codegen first ORM here. It is used as a deterministic SQL construction layer controlled by the semantic compiler pipeline. Flow: 1. LLM generates AST JSON. 2. AST is canonicalized and validated. 3. SQL handlers build clauses with jOOQ DSL + controlled SQL tokens. 4. SQL is emitted with named params. 5. JDBC executes with bind values. jOOQ conversion examples json , ], "groups": [] } } java // DefaultComparisonAstOperatorHandler return DSL.condition(" " + sqlToken(operator) + " ", field, context.nextParam(value)); sql ... WHERE ("t1"."request_status" = :p1 AND "t2"."zp_asr_team_notes" ILIKE :p2) json , , ], "groups": [] } } java // DefaultInAstOperatorHandler + DefaultBetweenAstOperatorHandler + DefaultNullCheckAstOperatorHandler field.in(params) DSL.condition(" BETWEEN AND ", field, context.nextParam(v1), context.nextParam(v2)) field.isNull() sql ... WHERE ("t1"."account_id" IN (:p1, :p2) AND "t1"."requested_at" BETWEEN :p3 AND :p4 AND "t0"."zp_disconnect_id" IS NULL) json ] } ] } java // DefaultRowNumberWindowFunctionHandler return "ROW_NUMBER() OVER (" + overClause + ")"; sql ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY "t1"."account_id" ORDER BY "t1"."requested_at" DESC NULLS LAST) AS "account_rank" JGraphT in Semantic Query JGraphT powers schema traversal and shortest join path resolution. It prevents LLM from inventing joins by computing paths from real FK metadata. Flow: 1. Graph engine refreshes vertices/edges from schema inspector FK joins. 2. Join path resolver chooses base table and initial required tables from retrieval. 3. SQL compile expands required tables from AST references (projections, where , having , exists , subquery , sort , group_by , time_range , metrics) before final join assembly. 4. Shortest paths are found with hop limits. 5. Resolved edges are de duplicated and returned to compiler. JGraphT traversal examples java // JGraphTSchemaGraphEngine.refreshGraph() for (Map row : joins) text Result: directed graph with forward + reverse FK edges for traversal flexibility. java // JGraphTSchemaGraphEngine.shortestPath() while (!queue.isEmpty()) for (String next : neighbors(current.node())) } } text Uses BFS style traversal; returns concrete SchemaEdge list for SQL joins. java // DefaultJoinPathResolver.doResolve() String base = candidates.get(0).name(); for (String target : requiredTables) return new JoinPathPlan(base, merged, requiredTables, unresolved, confidence); text Compiler receives resolved edges and unresolved table list; validator blocks unresolved joins. AST model capabilities Operators in AST model AstOperator.java includes: ILIKE BETWEEN IN NOT_IN IS_NULL IS_NOT_NULL Normalization behavior in AstFilter / AstSubqueryFilter also accepts aggregate style tokens and maps to base operators: COUNT_GT GT SUM_GTE GTE COUNT_NOT_IN NOT_IN _NOTNULL IS_NOT_NULL New AST nodes Boolean tree: AstFilterGroup.java Exists: AstExistsBlock.java Subquery filters: AstSubqueryFilter.java , AstSubquerySpec.java Windows: AstWindowSpec.java Full v1 payload: SemanticQueryAstV1.java Correlated field reference rule For exists.where and subquery_filters.where , a value starting with $ is treated as an outer field reference, not a string literal. $disconnectId means "compare against outer query field disconnectId " Resolver maps that field using outer entity semantic field mapping + outer aliases Example AST: json ], "groups": [] }, "not_exists": false } ] } Compiled SQL shape: sql EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM zp_request ex1 LEFT JOIN zp_disconnect_order exj2 ON ex1.zp_request_id = exj2.zp_request_id WHERE exj2.zp_disconnect_id = t0.zp_disconnect_id ) Validation coverage DefaultAstValidator.java validates: entity and field existence operator and value compatibility ( ILIKE string only, BETWEEN two values, IN / NOT_IN array) exists blocks subquery filters window specs groupBy/metric constraints SQL rendering coverage LIKE / comparison : DefaultComparisonAstOperatorHandler.java BETWEEN : DefaultBetweenAstOperatorHandler.java IN / NOT_IN : DefaultInAstOperatorHandler.java IS_NULL / IS_NOT_NULL : DefaultNullCheckAstOperatorHandler.java ROW_NUMBER window: DefaultRowNumberWindowFunctionHandler.java DATE_TRUNC status Not active yet: Placeholder exists in DefaultFunctionClauseHandler.java Missing concrete AST field + validator + runtime/handler : DbSemanticQueryToolHandler receives db.semantic.query . 2. runtime/core + runtime/pipeline + runtime/stage/ : SemanticQueryRuntimeService runs stage DAG. 3. retrieval/ : ranks
